paragraphs.body: I hear my mom calling, “Dinner is ready!” I am stoked because of how hungry I was. My four siblings and I all rush downstairs. We looked and were extremely disappointed with what we saw. The food was not ready, and my mom was still cooking. We had no idea what was going on and why she called us for dinner. She starts giving my siblings and I a task to complete to get dinner ready. We were starving and the last thing we wanted to do was help, but the smell of the food my mom was preparing encouraged us to do our tasks. My siblings and I start getting to work. My little sister and I started setting the table for dinner. We set it with all the silverware, cups, plates, and anything else needed for dinner. My three older siblings started cleaning or helping my mom cook to speed up the process. We were all very hungry, so we were trying our best to do the tasks as quickly as possible. Once we finished, we all sat at the table and talked to each other. We had this rule that no phones are allowed at the table, so the only thing we could do is talk to each other. We usually talked about what we did that day or something that’s on our mind. Then around that time is when my dad would come home from work. He usually likes to joke around and mess with my mom or my older sister. It was always funny. Dinner would finally be done by that time and that’s when it was the quietest. Each sibling would grab their own plates, and we created a line like the lunch line in high school. My mom would serve the food to us like the lunch lady and then we would take our seats for dinner. We prayed before every meal and then we started to eat. The only thing that you could hear is the scraping of the form of what my mom cooked, which was usually pasta with something. Then when everyone was about done is when the “adult discussions” would start. This is when my little sister and I would not talk because we did not know what they were talking about. My dad would usually bring up something about politics or some other controversial topic. This was always the most heated moment from dinner because a dinner full of opinionated Italians never ends well. Someone would leave angry just from the discussion. Sometimes my little sister and I would look and just smile at each other when tensions were high. Then everyone would get up and grab their plates and bring them to the sink where my mom would wash the dishes. One night after soccer practice I went to my friend’s house for dinner. This was the first time I went to his house, and I was excited. We had a tough practice, so I was excited to eat some dinner. When we arrived at his house his mom was in the kitchen cooking, so we went downstairs in the basement where he had a huge TV. We started playing video games and everything felt normal. Then his mom called us up for dinner. I looked at their dining table and it was empty, so I thought it was like what my mom would do and help to set the table. I was wrong. There were two plates on the counter, and she said enjoy your food. I was confused because she was not going to eat with us. Where were we going to eat? My friend grabbed our plates and said follow me. We went back to the basement and ate our food. This was the first time I experienced this. In Italy we would always eat at the table with the whole family there. I was shocked to see a different culture’s experience. In the US people are too focused on what they want next and do not appreciate the things they have. This was also started by the belief of the American dream. The American dream is a thought where people need to work hard to have a good life. It is based on the ideology that money is what brings happiness. I am not saying that money is not important or that it does not bring happiness, but it is not at the root of how you become truly happy. Sacrificing for the people you love is how you truly become happy. Working hard for the people that you love inspires you to work harder. My grandparents were immigrants from Italy, and they came to the US to start their own bakery. They started with very little money and only had each other. They had to work long hours and a very tough job to make a living. My dad was born later, and he started working at the bakery as well. They worked hard for many years and did it all for their kids, my dad and my aunt. This sequence followed with my dad now. He works for a software company making software for hospitals. He works very hard every day to bring his family joy. He is the main breadwinner in my family and works long hours. My mom is a college professor for the University of Pittsburgh. She works hard everyday cooking for all the kids and cleaning after them. My dad always wanted her to stop working, but she never wanted to because of how much she likes it. She sacrifices so much of her time cooking and cleaning, so the house is clean and there is a meal for everyone. Everyone in my family works hard for the next generation to be better. I am extremely grateful for all they do and the sacrifices for me to have a good life. Seeing my family work hard for each other encourages me to work harder. I always believe in helping others, especially family, so sacrificing some of my time from my days is nothing. It will all be worth it at the end because you make yourself and others happy. When someone says sacrifice, I hear a positive connotation. Some people may see it like that they are forced to do something, but I see it as helping others. In my opinion, helping others can help someone become happy. Seeing another person smile and being thankful for something that I did makes me happy. This is why I want to sacrifice to help provide for my family. My dad always tells me that I have to study and work hard, so I can give my kids in the future what I had growing up. I truly believe in this statement because I am truly grateful for the way I grew up. This is why I want to improve myself every day. I want my future kids to see someone they look up to like I see my dad. In Italy, I was invited to my friend’s house for dinner. This was around two years after my experience with my friend in the US. This was a completely different experience. When I arrived, his mom was still cooking, and she asked me if I could help set the table. This felt like home because my mom made me do the same thing. When she was done, she called all her kids and her husband to the table, so we could eat dinner. Now this was a familiar dinner for me. The two culturally different dinners prove to me what the different societies think is more important. In Italy the community is very close and open. Family is very important to the daily lives of an Italian. In the US family is more private and much more separate than in Italy. Joshua J. Fox once stated that, “Family is not an important thing. It’s everything.” Fox, a Canadian-American, wants the reader to know that family is not something that is just there for some events, it is that family is everything in your life. Family is there when you are born and when you die.

annotations.comment: Keep your verb tenses consistent! If you start in present tense, then stay in present tense. Or, switch everything to the past tense :)

annotations.text: heated moment from dinner because a dinner full of opinionated Italians never ends well. Someone would leave angry just from the discussion. Sometimes my little sister and I would look and just smile at each other when tensions were high.
My dad would usually bring up something about politics or some other controversial topic.
annotations.comment: Remember that a narrative is about one moment in time. If you use a phrase such as "would usually," then you're summarizing lots of dinners and conversations rather than describing, in concrete and specific detail, one specific time.

paragraphs.body: “Now class. I want you guys to think of some questions I am going to ask you.”

Who is your superhero?” “Man, I have never really thought of that.” “Who are the people that take care of you when you’re sad?” “I guess that’s my mom, but that also could be my sister.” “Who is the person that takes care of you the most?” “That would be my mom or dad.” “Who has sacrificed for you the most?” “My family.” So, when I arrive from work and see the table set with my wife cooking and my kids sitting at the table. I will remember my family dinners when I was a kid. I want my kids to talk and joke about our days. I want my kids to live in a house where they feel comfortable and own the things they want. I want to work hard to give my kids what they want, and I want them to work hard to give themselves what they want. I want to look at my house that I bought for my family and be proud. I did all of that with the people from my past that sacrificed their lives so that I could have the life that I have and will have. And I hope that my kids believe in family and also the importance of sacrificing from the people you love.

I want to work hard to give my kids what they want, and I want them to work hard to give themselves what they want. I want to look at my house that I bought for my family and be proud. I did all of that with the people from my past that sacrificed their lives so that I could have the life that I have and will have. And I hope that my kids believe in family and also the importance of sacrificing from the people you love.
annotations.comment: Lovely. Such an inviting and engaging tone. I love the repetitions here, too. Nice job :)

Great revision! A clear central focusing idea. Excellent details. Nice voice. Is this essay, at its heart, exploring money and happiness or is it exploring family togetherness and happiness? Also, remember to keep your verb tense consistent :) Great work!
